MATH 3190 Homework 10

Due April 16, 2026
Focus: Notes 11
Total Points: 45 + 10 (Self-Critique Comment and Uploading to GitHub) = 55 Name: Melanie Jensen

Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_10 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.

Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (45 points)

In the itsmr package is the dataset called wine that contains 142 accounts of Australian red wine sales per month from January 1980 to October 1991.

Part a (5 points)

Load in this data and convert it to a tsibble. You can do this by first making it a ts object by typing ts(wine, deltat = 1/12, start = 1980) and then piping that into the as_tsibble() function in the tsibble package.

Then use ggplot() to plot the time series. Show both the points and lines connecting them. On the \(x\) axis, put each year using the scale_x_yearmonth() function and give the plot a title.

wine_tsibble <- ts(wine, deltat = 1/12, start = 1980) |> 
  as_tsibble()

# Plotting the single series
ggplot(wine_tsibble, aes(x = index, y = value)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkred") + 
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_yearmonth(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") + 
  labs(title = "Australian Red Wine Sales", 
       x = "Year", 
       y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()

Part b (2 points)

Remove the last 34 rows from the wine data. We will use them to compare how our forecasts look later.

n <- nrow(wine_tsibble)

wine_train <- wine_tsibble[1:(n - 34), ]
wine_test <- wine_tsibble[(n - 33):n, ]

Part c (5 points)

Use the gg_season() plot in the ggtime library to plot the seasonality. Does it seem like there is trend and/or seasonality in this time series? If so, explain the patterns.

gg_season(wine_train, y = value, labels = "both") +
  labs(title = "Seasonal Plot", x = "Date", y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()

There appears to be seasonality as each year sales spike over the summer months. There is also evidence of trend where not only is each year over the last, but we can see that the end of a year is always more than the start of that respective year.

Part d (4 points)

Use the adf.test() function in the tseries library to perform a Dickey-Fuller test to determine if we have evidence this time series is stationary. Only the column with the sales should be entered. Since we have data by month, use the k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

adf.test(wine_train$value, k = 12)

    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  wine_train$value
Dickey-Fuller = -1.6093, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.7387
alternative hypothesis: stationary

With 95% confidence, we do not have enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis, meaning we do not have enough evidence to say that the time series is stationary.

Part e (6 points)

Difference the data once. Take \(Y_t = Y_{t+1} - Y_{t}\) for \(t=1,\dots,n-1\). In the notes, I defined the differences as \(Y_t = Y_{t} - Y_{t+1}\), but it actually does not matter. Plot this differenced time series (you can just put the index on the x-axis) and use the adf.test() function again on the differenced data. Again, use k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

wine_diff <- diff(wine_train$value)

plot(wine_diff, type = "l", main = "Differenced Wine Sales", ylab = "Diff Value")
adf.test(wine_diff, k = 12)
Warning in adf.test(wine_diff, k = 12): p-value smaller than printed p-value

    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  wine_diff
Dickey-Fuller = -5.0592, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary

When using the differenced value, with 95% confidence, we have enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis, meaning we have enough evidence to say that the time series is stationary.

Part f (4 points)

Now use the auto.arima() function in the forecast library to fit an ARIMA model to this time series (put the entire original tsibble into this function, not the differences time series).

Feel free to check out this resource that discusses more about time series, including what the drift term is.

wine_mod <- auto.arima(wine_train)
wine_mod
Series: wine_train 
ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] with drift 

Coefficients:
         ar1      ma1     sma1   drift
      0.9144  -0.7519  -0.7247  9.0791
s.e.  0.0918   0.1356   0.1663  1.6526

sigma^2 = 33825:  log likelihood = -639.11
AIC=1288.23   AICc=1288.9   BIC=1301.05

Part g (5 points)

Use the forecast() function from the forecast library to predict the future for 34 months. You should specify you are using the forecast library by typing forecast::forecast() since the itsmr library also has a forecast() function. The input of this function is your model you fit in part (f). Use the model fit to the undifferenced data.

Then use the autoplot() function to plot the forecast.

wine_forecast <- forecast::forecast(wine_mod, level = 95, h = 34)

autoplot(wine_forecast) + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1980:1992)
Scale for x is already present.
Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale.

Part h (8 points)

Plot the actual values for 1989, 1990, and 1991 along with the predicted values from the forecast and a shaded 95% interval bands. The geom_ribbon() function may be useful here. Is there any point where the true value is outside the interval? If so, report the year(s) and month(s) where that is the case.

forecast_df <- data.frame(
  time     = as.numeric(time(wine_forecast$mean)),
  forecast = as.numeric(wine_forecast$mean),
  lo95     = as.numeric(wine_forecast$lower[, "95%"]),
  hi95     = as.numeric(wine_forecast$upper[, "95%"])
)

test_df <- data.frame(
  time  = as.numeric(time(as.ts(wine_test))),
  value = as.numeric(as.ts(wine_test))
)

combined_df <- inner_join(forecast_df, test_df, by = "time")

ggplot(combined_df, aes(x = time)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo95, ymax = hi95, fill = "95% CI"), alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = forecast, colour = "Forecast")) +
  geom_line(aes(y = value, colour = "Actual")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1989:1992, limits = c(1989, 1992)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Forecast" = "blue", "Actual" = "red")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("95% CI" = "blue", "Error" = "red")) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Wine Sales", colour = "Lines", fill = "Ribbons") +
  theme_bw()

There is only one point in which the actual value is outside of the 95% confidence interval for the forecast and that is at approximately march of 1991.

Part i (6 points)

Use the checkresiduals() function in the forecast library to create a residual plot, an autocorrelation plot, and a histogram for the residuals. The input to this function should be your model fit with the auto.arima() function. Comment on whether the assumptions seem to be satisfied here. If you’d like, you can make a QQ-plot as well, but the histogram contains the same information.

Note: this function will also give you information from the Ljung-Box test, which tests if the residuals exhibit autocorrelation. A large p-value means we have no evidence of autocorrelation.

checkresiduals(wine_mod)

    Ljung-Box test

data:  Residuals from ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] with drift
Q* = 19.463, df = 19, p-value = 0.4275

Model df: 3.   Total lags used: 22

The residual plot is showing no obvious patterns. While there is a little fanning occuring, where the variance is unequal, it does not look to be too bad. The autocorrelation plot is not showing any significant autocorrelation, as evidenced by the ljung-box test. The histogram is showing signs of being skewed to the left and having short tails.
